overall.description: A ship bridge simulator showing first-person navigation through open water at night. The vessel's bridge features three navigation monitors displaying nautical charts and radar, while the view through the window shows the ship's bow cutting through dark seas with occasional lightning flashes illuminating the horizon.

scene_state.functional_zone: ship bridge navigation station with forward viewport overlooking the bow

events.description: lightning flash illuminates the ocean surface on the starboard side
bright lightning strike visible on the right horizon

agents.ego_description: navigating the vessel from the bridge, monitoring the radar and nautical charts while proceeding through open water at night

entered.detail: leftmost display showing coastal map with depth soundings and navigation data
center display with rotating radar sweep showing surrounding vessels and terrain
rightmost blue-screen display showing vessel telemetry and systems data
forward deck structure of the vessel visible through the bridge window
dark open water surrounding the vessel with visible wave patterns
metal framing surrounding the forward viewport with support pillars

segments.scene_description: The segment shows a continuous first-person view from a ship's bridge at night. Three navigation monitors are visible at the top of the frame displaying charts, radar, and data while the vessel moves steadily through open water. Occasional lightning flashes illuminate the ocean on the starboard side, indicating stormy weather conditions.

merged_segments.scene_description: The shot maintains a steady first-person perspective from a ship's bridge as it navigates through a dark, stormy ocean. It begins with a view of three navigation monitors at the top of the frame, which display active nautical charts, radar, and vessel data. As the ship moves forward through the open water, the deep darkness of the night is periodically broken by sudden flashes of lightning. These bursts of light illuminate the starboard side of the ocean, revealing the turbulent sea before the environment returns to a heavy, nocturnal gloom.
